data.title: Volatility watch: Wingstop earnings could lead to a big share price swing
data.description:
Wingstop (NASDAQ:WING) is being closely watched ahead of its earnings report on February 19 due to a higher than normal level of options trading and short interest on the stock higher than restaurant sector peers.

On Wall Street, BTIG has Wingstop (NASDAQ:WING) lined up as a top pick ahead of the restaurant company's earnings report on February 19. Despite expecting a significant sequential deceleration in same-store sales from Q3, analyst Peter Saleh and his team remain optimistic about Wingstop's (WING) Q4 performance.

The firm expects Wingstop (WING) to see a deceleration from the sizzling 20.9% same-store sales growth in Q3, but easily top the consensus estimate for 10.6% growth. The reintroduction of the $16.99 Boneless Meal Bundle is expected to have provided a tailwind during the quarter.

Looking ahead to 2025, Saleh anticipates Wingstop (WING) to deliver mid-single digit same-store sales guidance, in line with long-term projections. He expects an acceleration in unit growth, potentially reaching the mid-teens, and forecasts 337 net new units, above the high-end estimates for 2024.

Saleh also pointed to supply chain benefits as a key factor in Wingstop's (WING) performance and other factors such as reduced wing cost volatility, decoupling costs from the spot market and capturing 400-600 basis points of benefit on food costs.

Looking further ahead, BTIG views Wingstop as a category-leading restaurant concept with best-in-class unit economics and robust unit development. Strong sales driven by national advertising, menu innovation, and delivery are seen continuing to contribute to the company's success. The firm believes Wingstop (WING) has established competitive advantages in technology, digital ordering, and development.

BTIG has a price target of $370 on Wingstop (WING) based on a 47.5x EV/EBITDA multiple applied to the 2025 adjusted EBITDA estimate of $242.5 million, reflecting a premium to the five-year average due to strong sales and unit growth trends. The average Wall Street price target on Wingstop (WING) is $356.60.

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha analyst Caffital Research warned that the risk-to-reward profile on Wingstop (WING) is now skewed to the negative side.

Options trading on Wingstop (WING) implies a share price move of more than 10% after the earnings report is released. As a reminder, Wingstop (WING) slid more than 20% after its Q3 earnings report.

data.title: Bank of America credit card delinquency rate edges up, net charge-offs slip in January
data.description:
Bank of America's (NYSE:BAC) credit card metrics came in mixed for January, as delinquencies rose and net charge-offs fell sequentially, the lender disclosed on Tuesday.

The BA Master Credit Card Trust II delinquency rate advanced to 1.48% from 1.46% in December and 1.35% a year earlier. That compares with the prepandemic mark of 1.61% in January 2020.

Its net charge-off rate of 2.48% compared with 2.51% in the prior month, 2.23% in January 2024 and 2.54% five years ago.

Lending activity retreated during the month, with receivables outstanding decreasing 2.5% M/M to $15.5B, the filing showed.

data.title: Chemours rises as quarterly loss narrows from year earlier
data.description:
Chemours’s (NYSE:CC) stock on Tuesday rose as much as 4.1% after the chemicals maker reported a smaller loss for the fourth quarter. Sales of refrigerant rose, while volumes increased for two of its business units.

Its quarterly loss was $8 million, or $0.05 a share, compared with a loss of $18 million, or $0.12 a share, a year earlier.

Excluding one-time items, adjusted earnings were $0.11 a share, missing the average estimate of $0.16 among Wall Street analysts.

Sales slid 1% from a year earlier to $1.36 billion, in line with the consensus estimate of $1.37 billion.

A 3% decline in prices, partly offset by a 2% increase in volume, led to the overall drop.

Sales fell in Chemours’s (NYSE:CC) advanced performance materials business. Volumes rose with the expansion of capacity at its Teflon coatings products, more than making up for slowing demand in the hydrogen market.

The titanium technologies unit also experienced a decline in volumes. Sales in its thermal and specialized solutions business rose on a 7% gain in volumes because of improved demand for Chemours’s (NYSE:CC) Opteon Refrigerant blends.

Management said the company has sought to soften the effect of lower prices by cutting costs and adjusting inventories.

data.title: GreenPower targets increased production efficiency with consolidated California operations
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: GreenPower Motor Company Inc. (NASDAQ:GP) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Fraser Atkinson highlighted a 35% quarter-over-quarter revenue increase, reaching $7.2 million, primarily driven by sales of 13 BEAST Type D all-electric school buses, one Nano BEAST, and 14 EV Star vehicles. He emphasized improvements in gross profit, attributing gains to higher output at the South Charleston facility in West Virginia and contributions from California and Oregon sales.
  • Atkinson noted the growing demand for electric school buses, citing surveys indicating strong parental support for electric options due to health and safety concerns. He underscored GreenPower’s unique position as the only OEM with purpose-built Class IV Type A and Type D electric school buses, supported by federal and state incentive programs.
  • President Brendan Riley announced production enhancements at the West Virginia facility, enabling delivery of one BEAST per week, with plans to increase to two units weekly by April, alongside Nano BEAST production. Riley also highlighted leadership changes, including the appointment of James Redd as West Virginia production manager, to improve productivity and efficiency.
  • Riley discussed the consolidation of California operations into a single facility in Riverside, citing expected benefits such as increased efficiency, cost savings, and improved collaboration between engineering and production teams. This move also positions GreenPower closer to its customer base and suppliers.
  • CFO Michael Sieffert reported steps to reduce overhead costs through the California consolidation, with expectations for improved management oversight and gross profit margins over time. He noted a 7.8% decline in SG&A expenses compared to the prior year due to cost management measures.

Outlook

  • Management anticipates increased output from the West Virginia facility and expects production efficiency improvements driven by the California consolidation. Riley stated, "These efforts are just the beginning of our story; dedication to continuous improvement of our cost, our quality and our output."
  • Atkinson highlighted ongoing federal and state funding developments, including $500 million allocated for electric school buses in California and $500 million for similar initiatives in New York. These programs are expected to support demand through 2025 and beyond.

Financial Results

  • GreenPower reported Q3 2025 revenue of $7.2 million, a 35% increase over the prior quarter. Gross profit for the quarter was approximately $1.05 million, representing 14.6% of revenues.
  • Revenue was generated from the sale of 13 BEAST Type D school buses, one Nano BEAST, 14 EV Star vehicles, and contributions from parts sales, leases, and the truck body division.
  • Management attributed gross profit improvements to sales in California and Oregon, partially offset by lower-than-expected margins in the truck body division and new West Virginia operations.
  • The company raised $3 million through a share offering in October, with proceeds allocated to electric vehicle production and product development.

Q&A

  • Craig Irwin, ROTH Capital Partners, inquired about state and federal funding for electric school buses. Atkinson explained that while federal funding remains fluid, state-level programs like California’s $500 million initiative and New York’s $500 million funding allocation provide strong support for electric school bus adoption.
  • Irwin asked about the sequential increase in SG&A expenses. CFO Sieffert attributed the rise to headcount increases and noted plans to reduce salary expenses through California operation consolidations.
  • Tate Sullivan, Maxim Group, questioned the active deployment of GreenPower's electric school buses. Riley stated that 10 of 100 vehicles from West Virginia had been delivered, with plans to ramp up weekly deliveries. He added that California deployments have been solid, with strong customer satisfaction and follow-on orders.
  • Sullivan also asked about gross profit margin improvements. Sieffert emphasized that higher throughput and operational efficiencies from the California consolidation and West Virginia facility upgrades are expected to drive margin gains.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, with questions focused on funding dynamics, production efficiency, and deployment progress. Irwin praised the quarter as "a great way to start calendar 2025," while maintaining a critical focus on cost controls and funding clarity.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing progress in operational efficiency and funding support. Atkinson highlighted GreenPower’s unique market position and production enhancements, while Riley expressed bullishness on the EV Star line and school bus deployments.
  • Compared to Q2, management’s tone reflected increased confidence due to revenue growth and operational advancements. Analyst sentiment remained neutral to slightly positive, tempered by concerns over funding fluidity and cost management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue increased by $1.9 million quarter-over-quarter, driven by higher vehicle sales across multiple models.
  • Gross profit margins improved significantly from 8.6% in Q2 to 14.6% in Q3, supported by increased output and sales mix.
  • SG&A expenses rose sequentially from $2.2 million to $2.9 million due to headcount increases, with management expecting reductions from operational consolidations.
  • Management’s tone showed greater confidence in production efficiency and funding stability compared to Q2, while analysts maintained a cautious stance, focusing on cost controls and funding clarity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged challenges in achieving higher gross profit margins, particularly in the truck body division and West Virginia operations. Mitigation efforts include operational consolidation and throughput improvements.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the fluidity of federal funding and the impact of consolidation-related expenses on near-term profitability. Sieffert assured that these costs are manageable and would not involve significant one-time charges.

Final Takeaway

GreenPower Motor Company showcased strong revenue growth and operational advancements in Q3 2025, fueled by increasing demand for electric school buses and efficiency improvements. With plans for expanded production capacity and operational consolidation, the company is positioning itself for sustained growth. However, funding fluidity and cost management remain critical areas to monitor as GreenPower progresses toward higher profitability and market penetration.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Woodside Energy in talks with at least three partners for stake in Louisiana LNG project - Reuters
data.description:
Woodside Energy (NYSE:WDS) has talked to at least three potential partners about acquiring a stake its Louisiana LNG project, Reuters reported Tuesday, looking to sell 50% of the project's Phase 1 of development.

The suitors include Japan's JERA and Tokyo Gas, as well as Saudi Aramco-backed MidOcean Energy, according to the report, citing multiple sources, one of which said U.S. pipeline operator Williams (WMB) also had discussions with Woodside (NYSE:WDS) about buying an equity stake.

Woodside reportedly is expected to make a decision soon and has signaled it may accept several bids as it seeks to sell 50% of the first phase of the LNG export project, expected to cost ~$16B to build.

The first phase of the Louisiana LNG project will have a production capacity of 11M tons/year of liquefied natural gas; upon completion of all four phases, the facility should have a total capacity of 27.6M tons.

Woodside (WDS) is said to be seeking liquefaction fees of $2.70-$2.90/MMBtu on 10-20 year deals, ~$0.20 above current market rates, and shorter agreements would be at the higher end of the range, according ot the report.

Louisiana LNG was called Driftwood LNG until it changed owners following Woodside's (WDS) $1.2B acquisition of Tellurian last year.

data.title: Fluor outlines $575M-$675M EBITDA target for 2025 with focus on Urban Solutions growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fluor Corporation (NYSE:FLR) Q4 2024

Management View

  • David Constable announced his transition to Executive Chairman effective May 1, 2025, with COO Jim Breuer assuming the CEO role. Breuer will focus on the next phase of the company’s strategy, "grow and execute," emphasizing revised growth targets and project delivery.

  • Revenue for the year reached $16.3 billion, reflecting a 5.4% increase compared to 2023. New awards totaled $15.1 billion, with 85% being reimbursable. The year ended with a backlog that is 80% reimbursable.

  • CFO Joseph Brennan highlighted a strong cash position with $3 billion in cash and marketable securities, operating cash flow of $828 million, and plans to repurchase $300 million in shares during 2025 under the share repurchase program.

  • John Regan, succeeding Brennan as CFO, emphasized the company's pivot to the "grow and execute" phase, targeting revenue growth of approximately 15% in 2025 and a new awards book-to-burn ratio well above 1.

  • COO Jim Breuer highlighted robust opportunities in Urban Solutions, including data centers, advanced technologies, and life sciences. For Energy Solutions, Breuer noted a focus on front-end engineering work, LNG projects, and nuclear power opportunities.

Outlook

  • Fluor provided a 2025 EBITDA guidance range of $575 million to $675 million, supported by operating cash flow expectations of $450 million to $500 million. Full-year EPS is projected at $2.25 to $2.75 per diluted share.

  • Expected growth includes a 15% increase in revenue, G&A expenses around $180 million, and segment margins of 4%-5% for Urban Solutions, 3.5%-4.5% for Energy Solutions, and 5%-6% for Mission Solutions.

  • The company anticipates a strong pipeline of opportunities, including $46.5 billion in EPC prospects over the next 12 months, led by Urban Solutions ($24 billion), Energy Solutions, and Mission Solutions.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EPS for Q4 2024 was $2.32 per diluted share, excluding gains from NuScale deconsolidation. Segment profit for the year reached $635 million, with adjusted EBITDA at $530 million.

  • Urban Solutions reported a Q4 profit of $81 million, driven by 20% growth in backlog to $17.7 billion. Energy Solutions saw a significant rise in segment profit to $63 million, while Mission Solutions reported $45 million.

  • Legacy projects required $81 million in funding during 2024, significantly reduced from earlier projections. Remaining legacy work backlog stands at $850 million.

Q&A

  • Michael Dudas, Vertical Research: Asked about Urban Solutions growth and data center opportunities. COO Jim Breuer discussed the potential scale of data center projects, ranging from $500 million to several billion dollars, and highlighted a master agreement with a leading technology provider.

  • Sangita Jain, KeyBanc Capital Markets: Inquired about NuScale monetization. CEO David Constable emphasized ongoing detailed negotiations with a strategic investor and reaffirmed NuScale’s leadership as the only US NRC-approved SMR technology.

  • Jamie Cook, Truist Securities: Questioned Energy Solutions margins and the back-end loaded 2025 guidance. CFO Joseph Brennan attributed initial margin softness to project transitions but highlighted expected improvement as Energy Solutions repositions.

  • Andrew Wittmann, Robert W. Baird: Asked about staffing levels and execution. COO Jim Breuer noted net growth in resources driven by quick ramp-ups for Urban Solutions projects and highlighted efforts to optimize global resource allocation.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on strategic transitions and backlog growth, while raising concerns about NuScale monetization and Energy Solutions margins. Questions often pressed for clarity on timing and execution.

  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, frequently emphasizing strategic progress and opportunities. Defensive responses emerged during discussions on project delays and legacy challenges.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management displayed a more measured tone, particularly in acknowledging execution risks and market uncertainties.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue grew by 5.4% year-over-year, with a significant increase in reimbursable backlog and improved cash flow performance. In Q3, new awards were noted at $2.7 billion compared to Q4's $15.1 billion for the full year.

  • Management’s tone remained consistent, though there was increased emphasis on project delivery and resource optimization during Q4.

  • Analysts’ focus shifted from concerns about legacy project overruns in Q3 to inquiries about growth drivers and execution risks in Q4, reflecting optimism about future opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges in Energy Solutions include client delays for FID decisions and increased insulation work at the LNG Canada project. Management noted efforts to resolve outstanding issues and maintain project timelines.

  • NuScale monetization has faced delays, with management emphasizing the need for a balanced approach to benefit Fluor and NuScale shareholders.

  • Analysts raised concerns about staffing adjustments and potential overcapacity as major projects wind down.

Final Takeaway

Fluor Corporation is transitioning into a new strategic phase, "grow and execute," with a focus on Urban Solutions growth and reloading Energy Solutions. The company provided a positive financial outlook for 2025, supported by a strong backlog and robust pipeline. Management’s emphasis on reimbursable contracts and selective project engagement aims to ensure sustainable growth. However, delays in NuScale monetization and execution risks in Energy Solutions remain key areas to watch.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Regeneron, Sanofi asthma therapy Dupixent under FDA review for rare skin condition
data.description:
• Regeneron (NASDAQ:REGN) and Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) announced Tuesday that the U.S. FDA agreed to review a potential label expansion for the company’s asthma therapy Dupixent on a priority basis for a rare skin disorder known as bullous pemphigoid.
  • An inflammatory condition mainly found in the elderly, BP is characterized by itch and blisters, and with no FDA-approved therapies, roughly 27,000 American adults suffer from the condition.
  • Dupixent, also known as dupilumab, is approved in more than 60 countries for multiple inflammatory diseases, including atopic dermatitis, asthma, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
  • The companies’ supplemental Biologics License Application ((sBLA)) is backed by data from a successful pivotal trial in which a patient population five times bigger than those on placebo witnessed lasting clinical remission from BM after receiving dupilumab.
  • In connection with the review, the FDA has issued June 20, 2025, as the target action date for the application.

data.title: NeoGenomics targets 1M patients annually by 2028 with 11%-13% revenue growth in 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NeoGenomics, Inc. (NASDAQ:NEO) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Chris Smith highlighted strong financial performance, with Q4 revenue reaching $172 million, marking nine consecutive quarters of double-digit growth. He also announced his retirement effective April 1, 2025, with Tony Zook set to assume the role, citing Zook's commercial expertise as a key asset for NeoGenomics.

  • Smith emphasized NeoGenomics' goal of serving 1 million patients annually by 2028, supported by increased penetration in community oncology and a broad oncology test menu. Investments in commercial resources and new product launches were key strategic moves.

  • CFO Jeffrey Sherman reported adjusted gross margins reaching 48%, their highest level in 20 quarters, and adjusted EBITDA of $12 million for the quarter. He also noted improved cash flow from operations of $10 million in Q4.

  • Chief Commercial Officer Warren Stone outlined robust growth in NGS testing, which saw 24% revenue growth in Q4 and now constitutes over 30% of total revenue. He also unveiled plans to expand the salesforce to 140 members, targeting deeper market penetration.

  • NeoGenomics announced a strategic collaboration with Adaptive Biotechnologies to enhance MRD testing for select blood cancers, reinforcing the company’s leadership in heme testing.

Outlook

  • For 2025, the company provided guidance of $735 million to $745 million in revenue, representing 11%-13% growth. Adjusted EBITDA guidance was set at $55 million to $58 million, reflecting a 38%-45% improvement.

  • Smith noted significant opportunities in the second half of 2025, attributed to the launch of the PanTracer liquid biopsy test and an expanded salesforce. He also highlighted expectations for 25% annual growth in NGS revenue, outpacing the market growth rate of around 15%.

  • The company anticipates achieving 100-150 basis points of annual gross margin expansion, accompanied by 250-300 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin growth.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue totaled $172 million, driven by a 9% increase in clinical testing volumes and a 5% rise in revenue per test. Adjusted gross profit increased by 14% year-over-year.

  • The NGS business grew 24% in Q4 and 34% for the full year, reflecting strong adoption of large test panels. Pharma services experienced softer performance in Q4 due to a lack of year-end budget flush, but stabilization and modest growth are expected in 2025.

  • NeoGenomics concluded 2024 with $387 million in cash and marketable securities, planning to use these funds to retire $201 million in convertible notes due in May 2025.

Q&A

  • Andrew Brackman, William Blair, asked about upside levers and risks for 2025 guidance. Smith cited opportunities in NGS, the PanTracer liquid biopsy launch, and the Adaptive partnership as key growth drivers. He acknowledged that challenges in the pharma business, including restrictions on RaDaR contracts, remain a risk.

  • Puneet Souda, SVB Leerink, inquired about AUP assumptions and gross margin impacts from the PanTracer product. CFO Sherman noted that AUP growth would likely mirror 2024 trends, while PanTracer is expected to be accretive to gross margins.

  • Jack Slevin, Jefferies, questioned the timeline for RaDaR 1.1 and competitive positioning in the MRD market. Smith confirmed that RaDaR 1.1 is on track for CLIA validation by early Q2 2025 and emphasized NeoGenomics' expertise in MRD innovation.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on growth opportunities such as NGS and liquid biopsy launches while raising concerns about challenges in the pharma segment. Questions reflected interest in competitive positioning and revenue drivers.

  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing achievements in revenue growth and operational efficiency. However, a more cautious tone emerged during Q&A when addressing pharma challenges and RaDaR litigation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue grew from $168 million in Q3 to $172 million in Q4, with adjusted gross margins improving from 47.8% to 48%.

  • NGS growth decelerated slightly from 26% in Q3 to 24% in Q4, reflecting tougher year-over-year comparables.

  • Management tone remained consistent, with a focus on sustainable growth and strategic investments. Analysts continued to raise questions about the pharma business, similar to Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing RaDaR litigation, with a trial scheduled for October 2025, as a significant risk. The inability to sell new RaDaR contracts has impacted pharma revenue growth.

  • Analysts raised concerns about the scalability of new product launches and the competitive landscape in NGS and MRD markets. Management's reliance on market dynamics and payer relations for reimbursement also presents uncertainties.

Final Takeaway

NeoGenomics delivered strong Q4 results, driven by growth in NGS and clinical testing volumes. The company outlined ambitious goals for 2025, including double-digit revenue growth and expanded product offerings like PanTracer liquid biopsy. While challenges in the pharma segment and RaDaR litigation persist, management’s strategic investments and market positioning in oncology diagnostics signal confidence in sustained growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Entergy projects >8% industrial sales CAGR through 2028 with $37B capital plan
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Entergy Corporation (NYSE:ETR) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Drew Marsh highlighted 2024 as a "transformational year," with adjusted EPS of $3.65 in the top half of their guidance range. The company has raised its capital outlook beyond 2025, simplifying their disclosures to note a long-term growth rate above 8% through 2028.
  • Industrial sales grew 15% in Q4 and 8% for the full year, driven by strong growth in the Gulf South region. Entergy added two hyperscale data centers to its outlook and announced a new electric service agreement with a large customer in Mississippi, with further agreements anticipated with Meta.
  • The company plans to invest $37 billion from 2025 to 2028 to support customer growth, enhance grid resilience, and expand its renewables portfolio. This includes 1,500 megawatts tied to Meta's expansion and modern gas plants under construction in Texas and Mississippi.
  • CFO Kimberly Fontan stated, "With the higher capital plan, we increased our equity needs by $300 million in 2026," while noting that $1.4 billion in equity has already been secured for 2025 and 2026. Guidance for 2025 remains unchanged, with an adjusted EPS range of $3.75 to $3.95 and expected weather-adjusted retail sales growth of 6%.

Outlook

  • Entergy projects industrial sales to grow at a compound annual rate of 12% to 13% from 2024 through 2028. Management noted strong customer interest in data centers, with 5 to 10 gigawatts of opportunity within their pipeline.
  • The company anticipates decisions on new customer-driven generation and transmission investments in 2025. This includes potential regulatory approvals for renewables and gas plants across multiple states.
  • Long-term adjusted EPS growth is now expected to exceed 8%, supported by the updated $37 billion capital plan.

Financial Results

  • Entergy reported adjusted EPS of $3.65 for 2024, in line with the top half of their guidance range. Industrial sales increased 15% in Q4 and 8% for the full year, contributing to 4% weather-adjusted retail sales growth overall.
  • The company highlighted strong credit metrics, with book FFO to adjusted debt at 14.7%. S&P upgraded SERI’s issuer credit rating to BBB from BBB-, which is expected to reduce borrowing costs.
  • Entergy plans to maintain healthy credit metrics, targeting a sustainable 15% FFO to debt ratio.

Q&A

  • Shar Pourreza, Guggenheim Partners: Questioned the drivers behind the $3 billion CapEx update and its implications for growth. CFO Kimberly Fontan explained that the increase reflects investments in generation, renewables, and incremental nuclear upgrades to support reliability and future customer demand.
  • Nicholas Campanella, Barclays: Inquired about the company’s approach to new nuclear opportunities. CEO Drew Marsh emphasized they are exploring a broad range of technologies, including SMRs and large-scale reactors, with a focus on managing risks and providing value to stakeholders.
  • Jeremy Tonet, JPMorgan: Asked about Entergy’s ability to finance incremental CapEx with limited equity needs. CFO Fontan attributed this to operational cash flow optimization, customer contributions, and pension-related benefits.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on Entergy’s ability to manage rising capital needs without significant equity dilution. Questions centered on new nuclear opportunities, CapEx funding, and large customer deals.
  • Management maintained a confident tone. CEO Drew Marsh frequently emphasized the success of their growth strategy and robust pipeline, while CFO Kimberly Fontan detailed the company’s disciplined financial management.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more assertive about long-term growth prospects, supported by clear advancements in industrial sales and capital planning.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management raised the long-term growth outlook in Q4, compared to Q3, where they had reported a narrower sales CAGR of 11% to 12% through 2028. The updated guidance now reflects growth above 8%.
  • CapEx plans increased by $2.7 billion in Q4, compared to the $7 billion increase reported in Q3, driven by additional investments in Mississippi and Louisiana.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from short-term regulatory developments in Q3 to long-term growth opportunities with large industrial customers and data centers in Q4.
  • Management’s confidence level improved notably, driven by successful execution of new agreements and an expanded capital plan.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified rising costs for turbine and equipment slots as a growing challenge, though they emphasized strong vendor partnerships and secured supply for future projects.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about potential delays in regulatory approvals for new nuclear and renewable projects, as well as the execution risks associated with large customer-driven investments.
  • Entergy’s ability to maintain favorable credit metrics amid higher CapEx and equity needs remains critical.

Final Takeaway

Entergy Corporation reported a strong close to 2024, with industrial sales and long-term growth prospects bolstered by a $37 billion capital plan through 2028. Management remains confident in their ability to capture additional growth opportunities, particularly in data centers and renewable energy. Analysts focused on the company's ability to balance rising CapEx with disciplined financial management, while questions about new nuclear opportunities and customer-driven investments highlighted key areas of interest for the future.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Grayscale launches new investment trust for Pyth token exposure
data.description:
Crypto asset manager Grayscale Investments broadened its crypto offerings with the rollout of the Grayscale Pyth Trust, providing investors exposure to the PYTH governance token (PYTH-USD) from the Pyth network, it said on Tuesday.

Pyth supplies price data to decentralized applications (dApps), pulling from traders and market makers, especially within Solana, where 95% of protocols rely on its feeds, according to Grayscale.

“The Pyth network plays one of the most significant roles in the Solana ecosystem,” said Rayhaneh Sharif-Askary, head of Product & Research at Grayscale. “By introducing Grayscale Pyth Trust, we aim to give investors access to additional higher-beta and higher-upside opportunities associated with the continued growth of Solana.”

The Pyth-invested trust is now open for daily subscription by eligible individual and institutional accredited investors.

Last month, the company launched a fund centered on Dogecoin (DOGE-USD)
